name: Zig Allocator API
summary: Zig's convention that there is no default global allocator: data structures accept a `std.mem.Allocator` parameter and allocation failure is an ordinary `error.OutOfMemory` path. The strictest mainstream example of explicit allocator plumbing.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Zig is the strictest mainstream example of language-level allocator plumbing: there is no default global allocator. Data structures accept a `std.mem.Allocator` parameter, allocation failure is an ordinary `error.OutOfMemory` path on the return type, and libraries do not secretly pick a heap policy. The cost is allocator parameters percolating through call graphs; the benefit is full transparency — at every line of Zig code that allocates, you can see *which* allocator it goes to.

This is one of seven complementary models in the chapter 7.12 family (Odin, C3, Jai, Beef, Hare, D), each making allocation policy visible at the language/library boundary rather than treating `malloc` as ambient global state.

sections.title: Standard allocator palette and calling convention
sections.content:
The standard palette is deliberately small: `page_allocator` (raw `mmap`), `FixedBufferAllocator` (bump on a caller-provided buffer), `ArenaAllocator` (bulk-free arena), debug/general-purpose allocator with leak/UAF detection, `SmpAllocator` (multithreaded), `c_allocator` (libc malloc shim). User code allocating a `std.ArrayList` writes `try std.ArrayList(T).initCapacity(allocator, 100)` — the allocator is a value passed alongside other arguments.

Zig chooses verbosity over hidden allocation: testing routines often take an allocator parameter so test setup can pin a `FailingAllocator` to deterministically exercise OOM paths. The same source can drive a debug build with leak detection, a release build with `c_allocator`, and an embedded build with `FixedBufferAllocator` purely by choice of the allocator passed in at the entry point.
